questions.stem: In a deep learning interview, why is ReLU often preferred over sigmoid in hidden layers?
options: ReLU reduces vanishing gradients and is cheap; sigmoid saturates for large |z|
ReLU outputs probabilities in [0,1] while sigmoid is unbounded and unstable
Sigmoid cannot be differentiated anywhere; ReLU is smooth everywhere
ReLU is mandatory for output layers of every multiclass classifier
questions.explanation: ReLU’s non-saturating positive side helps deep nets train more reliably.

questions.stem: Backpropagation at a high level: what computation does it efficiently perform?
options: A forward-only search for the global minimum without using any derivatives
Chain-rule gradients of the loss w.r.t. each parameter via reverse-mode AD
Random perturbation of weights until train accuracy hits one hundred percent
Exact closed-form solutions for every nonlinear deep architecture
questions.explanation: Backprop applies the chain rule layer-by-layer to obtain parameter gradients.

questions.stem: Vanishing gradients: which symptom and cause pairing is most accurate to describe?
options: Gradients explode only in the last layer when learning rates are too small
Vanishing means the loss becomes NaN because the batch size is one
Early layers get tiny updates when saturated activations or deep products shrink grads
It only occurs with ReLU and never with sigmoid or tanh activations
questions.explanation: Products of small derivatives through depth can starve early layers of signal.

questions.stem: CNN versus fully connected for images: what inductive bias should you highlight?
options: CNNs ignore locality and treat every pixel as an independent global feature
Fully connected nets always beat CNNs on images when data is scarce
CNNs require sequential tokens; RNNs are the default for grid images
Local connectivity and weight sharing exploit spatial locality and translation structure
questions.explanation: Convolutions reuse filters across space, matching image structure efficiently.

questions.stem: RNN high-level idea: what problem setting are they classically designed to handle?
options: Sequential data where order matters and hidden state carries temporal context
I.i.d. tabular rows with no notion of order or temporal dependence
Only static image classification without any sequence structure
Unsupervised clustering of unordered sets with permutation invariance only
questions.explanation: RNNs maintain state across time steps for sequence modeling tasks.

questions.stem: Transformer attention (high level): what core capability should you state in interviews?
options: Attention forces strictly left-to-right recurrence with no parallelism
Tokens can attend to any other positions, modeling long-range dependencies in parallel
Transformers cannot use positional information of any kind by design
Attention replaces loss functions so gradient descent becomes unnecessary
questions.explanation: Self-attention mixes information across positions without sequential bottlenecks.

questions.stem: Dropout as regularization: what does it do during training versus inference?
options: Drops units only at inference to make predictions intentionally noisy
Deletes layers permanently after the first epoch they underperform
Randomly drops units in training; at inference uses full net with scaled weights/identity
Guarantees zero train loss by removing the hardest examples each batch
questions.explanation: Dropout averages thinned networks implicitly; eval uses the full network.

questions.stem: Batch normalization: what primary training benefit do practitioners usually cite?
options: Removes the need for any nonlinearity in deep networks entirely
Guarantees global optima for non-convex deep loss landscapes
Converts unsupervised pretraining into supervised labels automatically
Stabilizes activations’ distributions, often enabling higher LRs and faster convergence
questions.explanation: BN reduces internal covariate shift-like instability and eases optimization.

questions.stem: SGD versus Adam: what concise contrast should you give in an optimizer interview?
options: SGD uses raw gradients (often with momentum); Adam adapts per-parameter step sizes
Adam never uses gradients; SGD is a second-order Newton method only
They are identical updates with different names across frameworks
SGD is only for transformers; Adam is only for linear regression
questions.explanation: Adam’s adaptive moments often train faster; SGD+momentum can generalize well.

questions.stem: Softmax at the output: what does it produce for multiclass classification heads?
options: Unbounded real scores with no probabilistic interpretation ever
A probability distribution over classes that sums to one
Binary labels that are hard 0/1 without any temperature or logits
A single regression value for continuous targets only
questions.explanation: Softmax turns logits into a categorical probability simplex.

questions.stem: Exploding gradients: what practical mitigations should you mention quickly?
options: Always decrease depth until the network has a single linear layer
Remove the loss function so gradients cannot grow by definition
Gradient clipping, careful init, residual connections, and stable architectures
Exploding gradients only affect inference and never training updates
questions.explanation: Clipping and architectural fixes keep update magnitudes under control.

questions.stem: Why do residual (skip) connections help train very deep networks in practice?
options: They delete nonlinearities so every deep net becomes a linear map
They force each layer to reset weights to zero after every epoch
They replace backpropagation with evolutionary random search
They provide gradient highways and let layers learn residual corrections
questions.explanation: Skip connections ease optimization by improving gradient flow through depth.

questions.stem: Cross-entropy loss with softmax: why is it a default for classification interviews?
options: It penalizes confident wrong class probabilities and pairs naturally with softmax
It is only valid for regression and cannot use class labels
It ignores probabilities and only counts hard 0/1 mistakes
It requires unsupervised clustering before any supervised training
questions.explanation: CE drives predicted distributions toward the true one-hot (or soft) labels.

questions.stem: Learning rate too high versus too low: what failure modes should you describe?
options: Too high always finds better minima; too low always overfits instantly
Too high: divergence/oscillation; too low: painfully slow or stuck progress
Learning rate only affects inference latency, never training dynamics
Any nonzero learning rate guarantees convergence for deep nets
questions.explanation: LR scale controls step size stability versus training speed.

questions.stem: Weight initialization: why does poor init matter more as networks get deeper?
options: Initialization is irrelevant because batch norm deletes all scale effects forever
Deep nets ignore init and sample new random weights every forward pass
Bad scales cause activations/gradients to explode or vanish through many layers
Only the last layer’s init matters; hidden layers can start at zeros always
questions.explanation: Signal propagation through depth is sensitive to initial variance choices.

questions.stem: Max pooling in CNNs: what role does it typically play in the architecture?
options: Increases spatial resolution to recover finer pixel-level details always
Replaces convolutions entirely so filters are never learned
Converts classification logits into regression targets automatically
Downsamples feature maps, providing local invariance and reducing compute
questions.explanation: Pooling aggregates local neighborhoods and shrinks spatial dimensions.

questions.stem: Epoch versus iteration versus batch: how do you define them crisply in interviews?
options: Batch: one update’s samples; iteration: one update; epoch: one pass over the data
Epoch means one gradient step; batch means the entire dataset always
Iteration equals epoch; batch size must equal the full training set
These terms are interchangeable slang with no standard meanings
questions.explanation: Clear terminology: batch→step→epoch as nested training units.

questions.stem: Why is tanh sometimes preferred over sigmoid in hidden layers historically?
options: Tanh cannot saturate; sigmoid never saturates for large magnitudes
Zero-centered outputs can ease optimization versus sigmoid’s strictly positive range
Tanh outputs are probabilities summing to one across the layer
Sigmoid is undefined for negative inputs while tanh always is
questions.explanation: Zero-mean-ish activations historically helped compared with sigmoid’s bias.

questions.stem: Data augmentation for vision: what is the interview-level purpose you should state?
options: Replace labels with random noise to regularize via label smoothing only
Guarantee that train accuracy becomes one hundred percent every epoch
Synthetically expand diversity so models generalize beyond the raw training images
Remove the need for any convolutional inductive bias in the model
questions.explanation: Augmentation fights overfitting by exposing varied views of the same labels.

questions.stem: Transfer learning with pretrained CNNs: what practical advantage do you emphasize?
options: Pretraining always forbids fine-tuning of any layer by definition
It only works for language and cannot help image tasks at all
Transfer learning deletes the need for a validation set permanently
Reuse features learned on large data when your labeled set is smaller
questions.explanation: Pretrained features bootstrap performance when target data is limited.

questions.stem: LSTM/GRU motivation: what RNN failure mode do gated units primarily address?
options: Difficulty learning long-range dependencies due to vanishing gradients through time
Inability to process any sequential input shorter than two tokens
The requirement that all sequences must share identical lengths forever
Transformers’ quadratic attention cost on very long documents
questions.explanation: Gates improve gradient flow across many time steps versus vanilla RNNs.

questions.stem: Layer normalization vs batch normalization: when is LayerNorm often preferred?
options: Only when batch size is millions and sequence length is one
Variable-length sequences / small batches / Transformers where batch stats are awkward
LayerNorm requires identical batch sizes at train and serve always
BN is illegal in CNNs; LayerNorm is illegal in language models
questions.explanation: LayerNorm normalizes across features per example, fitting sequence models well.

questions.stem: Adam’s β1/β2 moments: what do they roughly track in the optimizer state?
options: Exact Hessian eigenvalues computed every step via second-order AD
The learning rate schedule milestones encoded as binary flags
Exponential moving averages of gradients and squared gradients per parameter
Dropout masks that must be identical across training and inference
questions.explanation: First and second moment estimates adapt step sizes per parameter.

questions.stem: Warmup + decay learning-rate schedules: why are they common for Transformers?
options: Force the model to ignore positional encodings during the first epoch
Replace Adam with SGD automatically after a fixed number of steps
Guarantee that validation loss decreases monotonically every update
Stabilize early training then anneal steps for finer convergence later
questions.explanation: Warmup avoids huge early adaptive steps; decay refines late training.

questions.stem: Multi-head attention: what benefit does using multiple heads provide conceptually?
options: Different heads can specialize in different relation/subspace patterns
It reduces attention to a single token so context is ignored
Heads delete the need for any feed-forward layers in Transformers
Multiple heads force the model to be strictly recurrent again
questions.explanation: Heads increase representational diversity of attention patterns.

questions.stem: Positional encodings: why do Transformers need them unlike classic RNNs?
options: Transformers store absolute positions in the optimizer state automatically
Self-attention is permutation-equivariant without explicit position signals
RNNs cannot represent order while Transformers natively know indices
Positional encodings replace token embeddings entirely in modern LLMs
questions.explanation: Without positions, attention treats bags of tokens as unordered.

questions.stem: Dying ReLU problem: what happens and what mitigations might you mention?
options: ReLU always dies after one epoch regardless of architecture choices
Dying ReLU means softmax outputs become uniform and untrainable
Units stuck outputting zero get zero gradients; Leaky/GELU/init/LR fixes help
It only occurs when using batch sizes greater than the dataset size
questions.explanation: Zero-gradient regions can kill units; smoother/leaky variants mitigate.

questions.stem: Teacher forcing in sequence models: what practice does the term refer to?
options: Forcing the learning rate to increase whenever validation improves
Replacing the decoder with a rule-based template system at inference
Labeling data automatically using a larger teacher model’s gradients
Training with ground-truth previous tokens rather than model predictions
questions.explanation: Teacher forcing stabilizes training by feeding correct history tokens.

questions.stem: Autoregressive generation: what does it mean for decoding at inference time?
options: Predict the next token conditioned on previously generated tokens iteratively
Emit the entire sequence in one non-sequential parallel shot always
Sample labels independently of any previous context or prompt
Optimize the encoder only while freezing the decoder forever
questions.explanation: AR decoding builds the output token-by-token from its own history.

questions.stem: Weight decay vs L2 penalty subtleties: what interview-level point is fair?
options: Weight decay always increases model capacity to fit noise better
In adaptive optimizers, decoupled weight decay can differ from naive L2 loss terms
L2 and weight decay are illegal to combine with dropout in any framework
They only apply to biases and never to weight matrices
questions.explanation: Implementation details matter: AdamW-style decay is not always identical to L2.

questions.stem: Skip-gram / Word2Vec era vs contextual embeddings: what changed conceptually?
options: Contextual models assign one vector per word type forever globally
Word2Vec requires Transformers; BERT forbids any co-occurrence statistics
Static word vectors vs context-dependent representations from deep encoders
Embeddings became unnecessary once one-hot encodings returned
questions.explanation: Modern encoders produce vectors that depend on surrounding tokens.

questions.stem: Convolution receptive field: why do deeper/stacked convs matter for vision tasks?
options: Deeper convs always shrink receptive fields toward single pixels
Receptive field size is fixed by the optimizer, not the architecture
Only the first layer’s receptive field matters for classification heads
Stacking expands the effective region of the image each unit can see
questions.explanation: Effective receptive field grows with depth, kernel size, and dilation.

questions.stem: Mixed precision training: what is the main practical motivation in interviews?
options: Faster throughput and lower memory using FP16/BF16 while keeping stable masters
It guarantees better generalization than FP32 for every architecture
Mixed precision deletes the need for gradient clipping permanently
It converts classification into regression by changing numeric types
questions.explanation: Lower precision speeds training/inference with careful loss scaling.

questions.stem: Mode collapse is usually discussed for GANs — what does the phrase mean?
options: The discriminator accuracy becomes exactly fifty percent forever
Generator produces limited varieties of outputs instead of covering modes
All batch norm running stats freeze and cannot update again
The latent space dimensionality collapses to one by mathematical law
questions.explanation: Collapsed generators fail to capture the full data distribution diversity.

questions.stem: Encoder–decoder vs encoder-only vs decoder-only: quick Transformer taxonomy?
options: All three are identical graphs with different marketing names only
Encoder-only models cannot use attention of any kind by definition
Enc-dec for seq2seq; encoder-only for understanding; decoder-only for generation
Decoder-only models forbid autoregressive next-token training objectives
questions.explanation: Architecture choice matches NLU, NMT-style, or LM-style task families.

questions.stem: Gradient accumulation: when do engineers use it during deep learning training?
options: Delete gradients every microstep so updates never happen
Accumulate validation metrics into the training loss automatically
Force the learning rate to equal the batch size at every step
Simulate larger batches when GPU memory cannot hold the desired batch size
questions.explanation: Sum micro-batch gradients before stepping to emulate big-batch training.

questions.stem: Label smoothing: what regularization effect should you describe accurately?
options: Softens hard one-hots to discourage overconfident softmax peaks
Replaces all labels with random classes each epoch for exploration
Smooths input images with Gaussian blur before every convolution
Forces train accuracy to match validation accuracy exactly
questions.explanation: Smoothing targets reduces overconfidence and can improve calibration.

questions.stem: Depthwise separable convolutions: what efficiency idea are you expected to know?
options: Remove all convolutions and use only fully connected layers
Factor spatial filtering per channel then mix channels with 1×1 convs
Apply one giant dense kernel across the entire image without sharing
Separable convs require RNNs inside each spatial location
questions.explanation: Factorization cuts parameters/FLOPs versus standard dense convolutions.

questions.stem: Curriculum learning: what training strategy does the term refer to in DL?
options: Train only on the hardest 1% of data from the first step always
Freeze the dataset order alphabetically and never shuffle again
Present easier examples first, then gradually harder ones as capability grows
Replace the loss with human letter grades instead of numeric losses
questions.explanation: Ordered difficulty schedules can stabilize learning on complex tasks.

questions.stem: Inductive bias: how do CNNs, RNNs, and Transformers differ at a design level?
options: All three share identical inductive biases and differ only in logos
Transformers cannot model sequences; RNNs cannot model any dependency
Inductive bias only matters for unsupervised clustering algorithms
CNNs bake locality; RNNs bake sequence state; Transformers bake flexible attention
questions.explanation: Architecture priors encode assumptions that help sample efficiency.

questions.stem: Senior DL: how do you diagnose whether a net underfits versus a data/label problem?
options: Check train metrics, capacity sweeps, label audits, and simple baselines before blaming depth
Always add more layers first because underfitting never comes from bad labels
If val beats train, the model is underfitting and needs heavier dropout
Underfitting is impossible for networks with more than three layers
questions.explanation: Separate capacity issues from data quality with controlled experiments.

questions.stem: When might plain SGD with momentum beat Adam on the final generalization gap?
options: Adam always generalizes best; SGD is obsolete for every modern benchmark
Some vision settings show SGD generalizes better after careful tuning/schedules
Momentum forbids using weight decay, so Adam cannot be compared fairly
SGD wins only when the dataset has fewer than ten examples
questions.explanation: Optimizer choice can affect generalization; don’t assume Adam is universally best.

questions.stem: Attention complexity: what scaling concern do interviewers expect you to mention?
options: Attention is always O(1) regardless of how long the context grows
Complexity is linear in n² parameters but constant in sequence length
Standard self-attention is O(n²) in sequence length for compute and memory
Only RNNs have quadratic cost; attention is strictly sublinear
questions.explanation: Quadratic attention motivates efficient variants and context limits.

questions.stem: BatchNorm at inference: what running statistics behavior should you explain?
options: Recompute batch stats from a single test example as if training
Disable all normalization layers completely at serving time always
Replace BN with dropout masks frozen from the last training batch
Use population mean/variance estimated during training, not per-batch test stats
questions.explanation: Eval mode applies learned/estimated global stats for stable serving.

questions.stem: Residual learning vs highway nets (high level): what shared idea connects them?
options: Give layers shortcut paths so deep stacks remain optimizable
Remove all skip connections to force each layer to be invertible
Replace gradient descent with closed-form least squares each block
Force every layer output to be a probability simplex
questions.explanation: Shortcut pathways are a recurring solution to deep optimization.

questions.stem: How would you explain the lottery ticket hypothesis in one interview paragraph?
options: It claims every random init fails and training is impossible without pretraining
Dense nets contain sparse subnetworks that can match accuracy when trained in isolation
It states dropout must equal exactly 0.5 for any network to converge
It proves Adam finds global minima for all non-convex deep losses
questions.explanation: Sparse winning tickets suggest overparameterized nets hide good subnets.

questions.stem: Feature collapse / representation collapse risks: where do they commonly appear?
options: Only in linear regression with two features and Gaussian noise
Never in deep learning because cross-entropy forbids constant features
Self-supervised contrastive/BYOL-style setups if invariance is poorly constrained
Only when using max pooling without any convolution layers
questions.explanation: Without proper objectives, encoders may map inputs to near-identical vectors.

questions.stem: Choosing activation functions today: what pragmatic guidance is interview-solid?
options: Always use sigmoid hidden layers for state-of-the-art Transformers
Activation choice never matters once batch norm is present anywhere
Only tanh is valid for language models by architectural law
ReLU/GELU/SiLU variants are common; match task norms and watch dying units
questions.explanation: Modern defaults favor smooth ReLU-family activations with empiricism.

questions.stem: Distributed data-parallel training: what synchronization idea should you mention?
options: Each GPU computes grads on a shard; all-reduce averages grads before the step
Each GPU trains a different architecture and never shares gradients
Only the rank-0 GPU sees data; others idle to save energy
All-reduce is replaced by writing checkpoints after every micro-batch only
questions.explanation: DDP keeps replicas in sync by averaging gradients across workers.

questions.stem: When is a smaller distilled student model preferable to the large teacher in prod?
options: Teachers are always cheaper to serve than students by definition
Latency, cost, and edge constraints dominate if accuracy drop is acceptable
Distillation only works offline and cannot produce deployable models
Students must have more parameters than teachers to retain knowledge
questions.explanation: Distillation trades a little quality for deployability and efficiency.
